lessonActivities.title: Zero Conditional – Facts & General Truths! 🎯
lessonActivities.content:
The Zero Conditional talks about things that are ALWAYS true! 🌍 Like scientific facts, general truths, and habits of the universe.

Structure: If/When + Present Simple, Present Simple

  • If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.
  • When you mix blue and yellow, you get green.

Key words: if, when, unless, as long as, provided that 💡
examples: When the sun sets, it gets dark. (general truth)
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 Zero Rule: Both clauses use Present Simple. No 'will' or 'would'!

lessonActivities.title: First Conditional – Real Future Possibilities! 🚀
lessonActivities.content:
The First Conditional is for REAL future situations that might happen! 🎲 We use it when we think something is possible or likely.

Structure: If + Present Simple, will + infinitive

  • If it rains tomorrow, I will stay at home.
  • If you study hard, you will pass the exam.

Alternatives: unless, as long as, provided that, when ✨
examples: If I finish work early, I will call you.
Unless you leave now, you will miss the train.
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 First Rule: 'If' clause = Present Simple. Result = will + verb. Never use 'will' in the if-clause!

lessonActivities.title: Second Conditional – Hypothetical Dreams! 💭
lessonActivities.content:
The Second Conditional is for UNREAL or IMAGINARY situations in the present/future! 🌈 We dream about what could be different.

Structure: If + Past Simple, would + infinitive

  • If I won the lottery, I would travel the world.
  • If I were you, I would study harder.

Special: Use 'were' instead of 'was' for all subjects (subjunctive mood!) 🎨
examples: If I had more time, I would learn Spanish.
If she were taller, she would play basketball.
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 Second Rule: Past Simple in 'if' clause, 'would' in result. Never use 'would' in if-clause or 'will' in result!

lessonActivities.title: Third Conditional – Past Regrets! 😔
lessonActivities.content:
The Third Conditional is for PAST situations that didn't happen! ⏰ We imagine how things could have been different.

Structure: If + Past Perfect, would have + past participle

  • If I had studied harder, I would have passed the exam.
  • If we had left earlier, we wouldn't have missed the flight.

Key: Both actions are COMPLETED in the past 📚
examples: If she had called me, I would have helped her.
If they had known about the party, they would have come.
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 Third Rule: Past Perfect in 'if' clause, 'would have' in result. Never use 'would' in if-clause!

lessonActivities.title: Mixed Conditionals – Time Travel Grammar! 🕰️
lessonActivities.content:
Mixed Conditionals combine different time periods! 🤯 One part past, one part present/future.

Common Mixes:

  • Past condition → Present result: If + Past Perfect, would + infinitive
  • Present condition → Past result: If + Past Simple, would have + past participle

Example: If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now. (past choice → present result) 🔄
examples: If I had woken up earlier, I wouldn't be so tired now.
If she were more careful, she wouldn't have crashed the car.
functions: Connecting past actions to present results
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 Mixed Rule: Combine Past Perfect with 'would' (present result) OR Past Simple with 'would have' (past result)!

writingActivities.question: Write 5 sentences about everyday scientific facts or general truths using the zero conditional.
writingActivities.hint: Use present simple in both clauses. Example: If you heat ice, it melts.

writingActivities.question: Write 5 sentences describing natural processes or habits using zero conditional (use 'when' or 'unless' in at least two).
writingActivities.hint: Example: When plants get sunlight, they grow faster.

writingActivities.question: Write 5 sentences about your possible plans for tomorrow using first conditional.
writingActivities.hint: Example: If I wake up early, I will go jogging.

writingActivities.question: Write 5 sentences giving advice or warnings to a friend using first conditional (include 'unless' or 'as long as' in at least two).
writingActivities.hint: Example: Unless you hurry, you will miss the bus.

writingActivities.question: Imagine winning a big prize. Write 6 sentences about what you would do using second conditional.
writingActivities.hint: Example: If I won a million dollars, I would buy a house.

writingActivities.question: Write 5 sentences giving polite advice to someone using second conditional (use 'If I were you' at least twice).
writingActivities.hint: Example: If I were you, I would apologize.

writingActivities.question: Write 5 sentences expressing regrets about things you didn't do last year using third conditional.
writingActivities.hint: Example: If I had saved money, I would have traveled.

writingActivities.question: Describe 5 past mistakes (yours or imaginary) and their consequences using third conditional.
writingActivities.hint: Example: If we had checked the weather, we wouldn't have gotten wet.

writingActivities.question: Write 6 sentences using mixed conditionals to explain how a past decision affects your present life.
writingActivities.hint: Example: If I had learned English earlier, I would be fluent now.

writingActivities.question: Create a short paragraph (8-10 sentences) about an imaginary 'what if' scenario linking past choices to current or future results using mixed conditionals.
writingActivities.hint: Example: If she had studied abroad, she would speak three languages today and would have better job opportunities.

gameActivities.question: Zero Conditional – Complete the fact:
gameActivities.hint: Present simple in both clauses – no 'will'!

gameActivities.question: First Conditional – Future plan:
gameActivities.sentence: If I ____ hard, I ____ the test tomorrow.
gameActivities.hint: Present simple + will. No 'will' in if-clause!

gameActivities.question: Second Conditional – Hypothetical:
gameActivities.sentence: If I ____ rich, I ____ buy a yacht.
gameActivities.hint: Past simple + would. Use 'were' for all subjects!

gameActivities.question: Third Conditional – Regret:
gameActivities.sentence: If she ____ earlier, she ____ the bus.
gameActivities.hint: Past perfect + would have + past participle.

gameActivities.question: Mixed Conditional – Past → Present:
gameActivities.sentence: If I ____ medicine, I ____ a doctor now.
gameActivities.hint: Past perfect for past condition + would for present result.

gameActivities.question: Third – No 'would' in if-clause:
gameActivities.sentence: If we ____ the map, we ____ lost.

gameActivities.question: Mixed – Present condition → Past result:
gameActivities.sentence: If I ____ more confident, I ____ the job interview last week.
gameActivities.hint: Past simple + would have for present → past mix.

quizActivities.question: Which conditional is used for general truths?
quizActivities.sentence: If you mix red and blue, you ____ purple.
quizActivities.hint: Zero conditional – present simple in both.
quizActivities.explanation: Zero conditional uses present simple for facts that are always true.

quizActivities.question: Choose the correct first conditional:
quizActivities.sentence: If it ____ tomorrow, we ____ the picnic.
quizActivities.hint: Present simple + will. No 'will' in if-clause!
quizActivities.explanation: First conditional for real future possibilities: present simple in if-clause, will + infinitive in main clause.

quizActivities.question: Second conditional uses:
quizActivities.sentence: If I ____ you, I ____ that job.
quizActivities.hint: Use 'were' for all subjects in hypothetical situations.
quizActivities.explanation: Second conditional: past simple (were for all) + would + infinitive. Common error: using 'was' or 'will'.

quizActivities.question: Third conditional structure:
quizActivities.sentence: If they ____ earlier, they ____ the accident.
quizActivities.hint: Past perfect + would have + past participle.
quizActivities.explanation: Third conditional for past regrets: past perfect in if-clause, would have + past participle in main clause. No 'would' in if-clause.

quizActivities.question: Which is a correct mixed conditional (past → present)?
quizActivities.sentence: If I ____ the course, I ____ fluent now.
quizActivities.hint: Past perfect for past action affecting present.
quizActivities.explanation: Mixed: past perfect + would for present result from past condition.

quizActivities.question: Common error – avoid 'will' in if-clause:
quizActivities.sentence: If you ____ me, I will help you.
quizActivities.hint: Never use 'will' in the if-clause of first conditional!
quizActivities.explanation: First conditional never uses 'will' in the condition clause – common ESL mistake.

quizActivities.question: Subjunctive in second conditional:
quizActivities.sentence: If she ____ here, she would help us.
quizActivities.hint: Always 'were' in hypothetical – even for he/she/it.
quizActivities.explanation: Subjunctive mood uses 'were' for all subjects in second conditional.

quizActivities.question: Third conditional – correct form:
quizActivities.sentence: If he ____ the train, he ____ on time.
quizActivities.explanation: Both parts refer to unreal past – never mix with present tenses.

quizActivities.question: Mixed – present condition → past result:
quizActivities.sentence: If I ____ more money, I ____ that car last year.
quizActivities.hint: Past simple + would have for unreal present affecting past.
quizActivities.explanation: Mixed type: present unreal condition (past simple) + past unreal result (would have + pp).

quizActivities.question: Identify the correct conditional type:
quizActivities.sentence: If plants don't get water, they die.
quizActivities.explanation: Zero conditional for scientific/general truths using present simple.

spellingActivities.hint: A sentence with 'if' that expresses a condition
Something that is imagined or supposed, not real
Feeling sorry about something you did in the past
The way something is built or organized
spellingActivities.sentence: Each conditional has its own grammatical ____.

spellingActivities.hint: The result or effect of an action
spellingActivities.sentence: The main clause shows the ____ of the condition.

spellingActivities.hint: A verb mood used for hypothetical situations
